moveAnnotations.annotation: We're playing the Bird Opening, an unconventional but solid way to fight for central control without playing e4. By advancing the f-pawn, we immediately influence the critical e5 square and prepare to support our center with moves like e3 or d4. This move gives us flexibility—we can later support a d4 advance or launch a kingside attack—while keeping Black guessing about our exact plans.

Black strikes at the center immediately, trying to prevent us from establishing a strong pawn presence there. This move challenges our setup and claims space in the middle of the board, forcing us to make a decision about how we'll respond to central pressure.

Now we define this as the Sturm Gambit—we're offering the d5 pawn a challenging choice by attacking it and preparing to control the d4 square. If Black captures with dxc4, we can recapture and maintain strong central influence; if Black holds the d5 pawn, we've challenged their control of the board and can continue developing our advantage with moves like cxd5 or Nc3. This aggressive approach fits the gambit's fighting spirit while keeping practical winning chances.
